payload.value:
Andrew 2026-05-10: 'could you have act actions in some separate repo in the same folder? Is that viable/preferable, especially for multiple agents working across environments — local, remote, etc.?' AND adoption-concern follow-up: 'I'm mostly concerned about this issue making act a non-starter for other users. I guess the other option is it gets its own repo within the main project repo? Is that functional at all?'

Today .act/ ops live in the project's git repo and ride the project's sync cadence. For multi-env scenarios (laptop + CC Web + Cowork agents working the same backlog), the act state needs to sync between agents quickly so claims and closes are visible. Coupling .act/ sync to the project's main-branch sync mostly works but couples two cadences that don't naturally match. Plus the resulting commit-log noise (~70% bookkeeping in this repo) is a meaningful adoption barrier.

Options to evaluate:

A) Status quo: .act/ in the project repo, in the working tree, committed normally. Forward fix at act-a659 (close op rides work commit) reduces noise to 1 act-op commit per task (the claim).

B) Separate-branch ops: .act/ on refs/act/ops in the same repo (a la git notes). Project main stays clean; tracker state syncs via a different ref. No submodule complexity but harder to inspect.

C) Submodule at .act/: act state is its own git repo, parent tracks a submodule pointer. Yes, git-within-git IS supported via submodules. Parent log: only submodule-pointer-update commits (which can ride work commits). Submodule log: noisy but invisible from parent. Tradeoffs: submodule UX is notoriously rough — clone --recurse-submodules, manual updates, divergent histories — that complexity might be worse than the noise it removes for adoption.

D) Separate sibling repo: .act/ is its own repo, .gitignored in the parent, lives at /<project>/.act/ as a sibling git working tree. Parent log: zero act commits ever. Tradeoff: tracker state doesn't travel with the project on clone — adopters have to clone two repos or run a setup step. Strongest decoupling.

E) External state: tracker lives outside the project entirely (~/.act-state/<project-id>/ or a remote service). Strongest decoupling. Loses 'git-resident' promise from the brief entirely.

Compare on: setup cost; multi-env sync ease; how cleanly the audit trail correlates with code commits; tooling friction (does act doctor still work? does git log -- on the project show act activity?); story for forks/PRs (does cloning the project bring tracker state with it?); ADOPTION FRICTION (lower is better — submodules historically hurt here).

Recommendation should land in docs/multi-env-design.md and feed an implementation issue if one of B/C/D/E wins. (A) is the deferral default; only switch if there's a clear win after factoring in adoption friction.
